artifacts.content:
Hi Mom,

I’ve been meaning to actually sit down and explain what’s been going on instead of just texting you bits and pieces. Things feel kind of precarious right now and I don’t want you to hear about stuff secondhand.

Work has been rough. I’m still on the Keppra 750 mg at 7 AM and 7 PM, but when I forgot my evening dose on December 14, 2024 and had that big tonic‑clonic seizure at my desk at Riverside Family Dental, Dr. Patel did a formal write‑up for “medication noncompliance” and put me on a 60‑day probation. That’s on top of the earlier warning from May 2024 when Karen Mills wrote me up because I missed three mornings in one month after nocturnal seizures and being too out of it from the Keppra + clobazam + Nayzilam combo to safely handle phones or charts. I hate feeling like the “unreliable” one when I’m trying so hard.

The seizures themselves are still all over the place. On January 18, 2025 I had one of my focal impaired‑awareness episodes at the front desk—froze, lip‑smacking, picking at my scrub top—and later Dr. Nguyen said a patient complained I looked “high or out of it.” And then there was the ER trip on November 9, 2024 after that prolonged tonic‑clonic at home when I missed my night dose finishing chart audits. The squad had to give IV meds on the way to OhioHealth Riverside Methodist, and now I’ve got a $3,200 bill in collections with Buckeye Recovery Services. I’m on a $75/month plan and you know I already had to borrow $500 from you in December just to keep up.

All of this is why I’m so focused on the upcoming visit about trying Epidiolex. I’m exhausted, but I’m still hopeful we can get things to a better place where I’m not constantly on the edge of losing my job or ending up back in the ER.

Love you,
Emily

metadata.subject: Trying to explain everything that’s been going on

artifacts.content:
Hannah,

Sorry to dump all of this on you when you’re already tired from Nationwide, but my brain is spinning and you’re the only one who really sees all the pieces.

I’m still dragging from the clobazam change. Ever since Dr. Nguyen at OSU Family Medicine bumped my nighttime Onfi to 20 mg back in August 2024 (still 10 mg in the morning), I’m so groggy that I’ve opened the front desk late three times. Remember that meeting with Dr. Patel on September 9 when she basically said if I’m late again they’ll have to cut my hours? Combine that with the May 2024 written warning from Karen about missing those three mornings after nocturnal seizures, and I feel like I’m always one bad night away from losing the job.

The morning jerks aren’t helping either. On February 3, around 7:15 a.m. in our kitchen, I had that strong myoclonic jerk in my right arm while pouring coffee, dropped the mug, cut my hand, and soaked the $1,050 rent check you’d already written. That was after staying up late doing end‑of‑month billing for Riverside again. I know you said it was “just paper,” but I still feel awful.

And then July 4 at Goodale Park… you had to give me the Nayzilam again after I had three focal seizures in an hour during the fireworks, freaked out all those families, and you ended up missing your AA meeting at the King Avenue community center. I still replay that night and feel guilty every time you grab your keys.

I’m going to ask Dr. Mehta if Epidiolex might let us back off the clobazam a bit so I’m not such a zombie in the mornings. I just want you to have your own life again too.

– E

Seizure / mood / life notes – for myself before next neuro + therapy weeks

1) Meds + money
- Still on Lamotrigine 150 mg twice daily. Need to remember what happened when I tried to stretch it in March 2024 because I couldn’t afford the $85 copay at the High Street CVS. I tapered myself for about two weeks, then had a cluster of four seizures in one day. Dad (Mark) drove up from Dayton on March 23 to help cover rent and pick up the refill. I cannot do that again.
- On top of that, the ER visit at Riverside Methodist on November 9, 2024 (two back‑to‑back tonic‑clonics after missing my night dose) left me with that $3,200 bill now with Buckeye Recovery Services. $75/month payment plan, plus the $500 I borrowed from Mom in December. Need to ask about Epidiolex patient‑assistance so I don’t drown.

2) Driving / court stuff
- Still thinking about the seizure‑related accident on North High near campus on May 3, 2022—Franklin County Municipal Court suspension for 12 months and $1,800 in fines/fees. I’m still paying that off. Also the March 18, 2023 I‑71 near Hudson seizure/fender‑bender that led to another suspension and the warning from Officer Daniels. Goal: if Epidiolex works, push hard for a solid 6‑month seizure‑free stretch so I can realistically reapply.

3) Mood
- Remember how bad things got after the Keppra increase in August 2023: three weeks of passive suicidal thoughts, started weekly 6 p.m. Thursdays with Dr. Grant at Riverside Mental Health. Need to ask whether Epidiolex could rock the boat mood‑wise and whether Dr. Shah should tweak sertraline first.

4) Independence
- Still hate that my meds (clobazam + leftover oxy from March 2022 wisdom‑teeth) live in the metal cash box in Hannah’s closet since I double‑dosed on January 9, 2023. Long‑term hope: enough stability that I can manage my own pill organizer again.

Hey Jason,

Figured I’d write a longer update instead of just sending you dog pics (though Oscar is currently upside‑down on the couch snoring like a chainsaw).

Seizures are still a mess. The big one on Christmas Eve at Mom and Dad’s really shook me. Dad started yelling in the kitchen about my medical bills and that old joint credit card he opened with my SSN back when I was at Columbus State, and I got that awful déjà vu + rising stomach aura. Next thing I remember is waking up on the kitchen floor with my tongue bitten and pee on my jeans, and Mom crying. I keep thinking about how stress seems to flip a switch in my brain.

I’m also still haunted by the stairwell seizure at 1437 back in October 2023, when I hit my head on the railing and Mike Thompson from 3A found me bleeding and called 911. Ever since, Mom and Dad have been pressuring Hannah to “keep an eye on me” or even let one of them move in. I know they mean well, but it makes me feel like a child.

On top of that, there’s the mental health side. Remember the three‑day psych stay at OSU Harding in April 2021 after those nocturnal tonic‑clonics and the suicidal thoughts? I still see Dr. Melissa Grant at the Harding outpatient clinic every other Tuesday at 3 p.m. for anxiety/depression fallout from all of this. I’d love to get to a place where I don’t need such frequent appointments.

Work is another stressor—between the November 5, 2022 focal seizure at Riverside where I wandered into sterilization and knocked over about $1,200 of CAD/CAM crown blocks (hello, HR write‑up) and the more recent January 12, 2025 break‑room seizure and warning from Karen that Dr. Patel might cut my hours if there’s “another episode on the clock,” I feel like I’m always one incident away from disaster.

I’m pinning a lot of hope on trying Epidiolex with Dr. Mehta later this month. Not expecting miracles—just fewer ER trips, fewer write‑ups, and maybe enough control to start thinking about my own place someday.

Love you,
Em

metadata.subject: Actual life update (not just Oscar spam)

artifacts.content:
Event: Neurology follow‑up – discuss Epidiolex + work/driving/money

Date/Time: Wednesday, February 19, 2025, 2:30–3:15 p.m.
Location: OSU Wexner Medical Center – Neurology Clinic
Attendees: Me, Dr. Mehta

Notes to bring up (don’t forget this time):

1) Work incidents
- December 14, 2024: missed PM Keppra 750 mg dose → tonic‑clonic at my desk at Riverside Family Dental. Dr. Patel wrote me up for “medication noncompliance” and put me on 60‑day probation.
- January 12, 2025: focal impaired‑awareness seizure in break room, knocked over sterilization tray. Karen Lewis gave written warning, said if I have “another episode on the clock” Dr. Patel might cut my hours.
- November 5, 2022: wandering focal seizure into sterilization room, knocked over ~$1,200 CAD/CAM crown blocks → formal HR write‑up.

Ask if he can write a general letter about my epilepsy/fitness for work in case my job is at risk.

2) Mood history
- Keppra increase August 2023 → 3 weeks of passive suicidal thoughts, started weekly therapy with Dr. Melissa Grant at Riverside Mental Health Center (Thursdays 6 p.m.).
- April 2021: 3‑day psych hospitalization at OSU Harding after cluster of nocturnal tonic‑clonics + suicidal thoughts; now seeing Dr. Grant at OSU Harding outpatient every other Tuesday at 3 p.m.

3) Driving/court
- May 3, 2022 seizure‑related car accident on North High near campus → Franklin County Municipal Court 12‑month license suspension + $1,800 fines/fees (still paying). Also March 18, 2023 I‑71/Hudson seizure/fender‑bender and license suspension; Officer Daniels’ warning.

4) Money
- November 9, 2024 Riverside Methodist ER visit (two tonic‑clonics) → $3,200 bill now with Buckeye Recovery; $75/month plan; borrowed $500 from Mom in December. Ask about labs/monitoring costs + Epidiolex assistance programs.

5) Meds/other
- Clarify interactions with sertraline (started March 2023 after Feb 18 ER cluster), Nexplanon (placed March 2024 by Dr. Moreno after pregnancy scare), clobazam lockbox situation after 1/9/23 double‑dose.
- Long‑term goals: 6‑month seizure‑free stretch to reapply for license; eventually work 30 hrs/week consistently; maybe taper sertraline if mood improves.

Hey Maya,

Checking in like you asked. Today’s been… a lot.

This morning I had another one of those focal impaired‑awareness seizures at Riverside. I was alone at the front desk, checking in a patient, and apparently I froze, started lip‑smacking, and picking at my scrub top with my right hand again. It felt like losing a chunk of time. Later Dr. Nguyen said the patient complained that I seemed “high or out of it,” which is mortifying.

All of it brings up old stuff: the seizure in the stairwell at 1437 in October 2023 when I hit my head on the railing and woke up to paramedics because Mike from 3A called 911; the three‑day psych stay at OSU Harding in April 2021 after the cluster of nocturnal tonic‑clonics and suicidal thoughts; even the Grace Community Church thing where I had a brief focal seizure during the June 4, 2023 8:30 a.m. service and Pastor Allen pulled me aside later to ask if I was “stable enough” to keep helping with the 2–4‑year‑olds in the nursery. It feels like everywhere I go, my brain makes people question whether I belong there.

I’m trying to hang on to some hope about Epidiolex. Dr. Mehta and I are going to talk through it in a couple weeks. I keep thinking: if it could cut the big tonic‑clonics down from 1–2 a month to just a few a year, maybe I could eventually get to that 6‑month seizure‑free window and not feel like such a burden when you or Hannah walk me home from Café Kerouac.

Anyway, thanks for listening to me ramble. If you’re still up for it, maybe we can do a low‑key lunch at that quiet place on High this Saturday? Somewhere I won’t feel like a spectacle if I blank out for a minute.

❤️ E

Hi Dr. Moreno,

I hope it’s okay that I’m emailing; I just wanted to give you a heads‑up before my next visit and ask a couple of questions I forgot at my last OB‑GYN appointment.

My neurologist at OSU Wexner, Dr. Mehta, and I are seriously considering starting Epidiolex soon because my seizures are still pretty frequent despite Keppra, clobazam, and lamotrigine. I’m trying to think through how this might interact with the Nexplanon you placed in my left arm last March at the OSU clinic, after that pregnancy scare when my period was two weeks late. I’m still not ready to tell my parents about that, but I am really grateful you switched me off the low‑dose pill because I am not in a place where an unplanned pregnancy would be safe.

I’ve been reading that some seizure meds can affect hormonal birth control, so I’m wondering:

1) Does Epidiolex change how effective Nexplanon is, or is it more of an issue with pills? Should I be using condoms as backup while we see how things go?
2) Could Epidiolex change my bleeding pattern again? Things finally settled into a kind of predictable light/spotty rhythm after a few weird months post‑implant.

Longer term, I’m still thinking about what we talked about regarding pregnancy “someday,” and planning any med changes 6–12 months in advance so I don’t end up in a situation like my cousin Megan at Grant Medical Center in April 2021 when she had that prolonged seizure during labor. Right now my main goal is just to get my seizures better controlled and avoid more ER trips like the November 9, 2024 Riverside Methodist visit, but I want to make sure whatever we do now doesn’t close doors later.

Thank you for any guidance you can give, and for being so patient with all my questions.

Best,
Emily Carter

metadata.subject: Questions about Nexplanon and possible Epidiolex start

artifacts.content:
From: emily.carter@gmail.com
To: mom.carter@example.com
Subject: Why I’m so focused on this new seizure plan
Date: February 10, 2026

Hey Mom,

I wanted to try to explain why I’m putting so much hope into the Epidiolex conversation with Dr. Mehta this month.

Honestly, one of my biggest goals for seizure treatment now is to get stable enough that I can safely taper off the sertraline. You remember when Dr. Amanda Lewis at Riverside Behavioral Health started it in March 2023, after that awful depressive episode and the passive suicidal thoughts I had? That all came right after the cluster of tonic–clonic seizures in the apartment and the OSU Wexner Medical Center ER visit on February 18, 2023. I never want to end up in that place again, but I also don’t love the idea of being on an antidepressant forever if my mood and anxiety would improve with better seizure control.

So when you hear me talking about fewer tonic–clonics or trying to cut back on clobazam or Keppra, it’s not just about numbers in a seizure diary. It’s about feeling like myself again and not living in constant fear of repeating February 2023. If we can get things steady enough, Dr. Lewis and I could at least discuss a slow, supervised taper instead of assuming sertraline is permanent.

I know you worry I’m aiming too high, but this feels like a realistic long‑term goal, not some magic cure fantasy.

Love you,
Emily

metadata.subject: Why I’m so focused on this new seizure plan

artifacts.content:
From: emily.carter@gmail.com
To: maya.thompson@example.com
Subject: Heavy stuff I didn’t say at lunch
Date: February 16, 2026

Hey Maya,

Thanks again for meeting me at the café today. There’s some heavier stuff I didn’t quite get out in person.

Long term, one of my goals for seizure treatment is to have things controlled enough that I can safely consider pregnancy in a few years. I told Dr. Patel at Columbus Women’s Health that I’d like kids someday, but I’m honestly terrified after what happened to my cousin Megan at Grant Medical Center in April 2021, when she had that prolonged seizure during labor. It scared our whole family, and I can’t get the image out of my head.

Dr. Patel and I did some preconception counseling about my meds, especially clobazam. She said that if I’m serious about trying for a baby someday, we’d need my seizures better managed first, and then plan any medication changes at least 6–12 months in advance. That timeline makes it feel very real and very far away at the same time.

So when I talk about hoping Epidiolex might help or cutting down on rescue trips to the ER, it’s not just about convenience. It’s about getting to a place where pregnancy wouldn’t feel like repeating Megan’s story. Right now it’s all very “future Emily,” but it helps me to have that as a long‑term target instead of just thinking about the next seizure.

Thanks for letting me unload this.

Love,
Emily

metadata.subject: Heavy stuff I didn’t say at lunch

artifacts.content:
Title: Prep notes for Feb 2026 neuro + big picture goals
Date: February 18, 2026
Location: Bedroom desk, blue notebook

– Main agenda with Dr. Mehta (OSU Wexner Medical Center):
  • Review seizure diary since Jan visit, esp. focal impaired‑awareness vs tonic–clonic.
  • Ask detailed questions about Epidiolex for focal seizures, interactions with clobazam, Keppra, lamotrigine, and liver labs schedule.

– Short‑term goals:
  • Fewer ER trips than last year (think about OSU Wexner Medical Center ER visit on Feb 18, 2023 after cluster of tonic–clonic seizures).
  • Reduce need for intranasal midazolam if possible.

– Mental health goal (MS1):
  • One of my biggest goals for seizure treatment is to be stable enough that I can safely taper off sertraline.
  • Remember: started sertraline with Dr. Amanda Lewis (psychiatrist at Riverside Behavioral Health) in March 2023 after depressive episode with passive suicidal thoughts following Feb 2023 cluster.

– Future pregnancy goal (MS2):
  • Long‑term: have seizures controlled enough to safely consider pregnancy without repeating what happened to cousin Megan at Grant Medical Center in April 2021 (prolonged seizure during labor).
  • Remind myself: already talked with Dr. Patel (OB‑GYN at Columbus Women’s Health) about preconception counseling and how clobazam might affect a baby.
  • Note: Dr. Patel said any med changes around pregnancy need planning at least 6–12 months in advance once seizures are better managed.

– Ask Dr. Mehta how realistic it is to aim for these MS1 + MS2 goals over next few years.

metadata.title: Prep notes for Feb 2026 neuro + big picture goals

artifacts.content:
To: Dr. Arjun Mehta <amehta@osuwexner.org>
From: Emily Carter <emily.carter27@gmail.com>
Date: February 3, 2026
Subject: Goals for treatment & questions about Epidiolex

Hi Dr. Mehta,

Ahead of my follow‑up later this month, I wanted to write out my goals for seizure treatment and a few questions about Epidiolex so we can use our visit time efficiently.

Another goal for my seizure treatment is to get my seizures controlled enough that I can safely return to driving so I don’t have to keep relying on my older sister Hannah to miss shifts at her job at the Nationwide Children’s Hospital billing department to drive me to appointments. Since my license was suspended for 6 months after the seizure‑related fender bender I had on High Street near the OSU campus on July 9, 2022, I’ve felt guilty about the extra financial stress it put on her when she had to pay the $450 insurance deductible and cover Uber rides for me for about three months.

More broadly, I’m hoping for at least a 50–75% reduction in seizures, especially the focal impaired‑awareness ones and the monthly focal to bilateral tonic–clonic events that sometimes land me in the ER at OhioHealth Riverside Methodist.

For Epidiolex specifically, I’d like to review:
– How well it tends to work for focal seizures
– Interactions with clobazam, Keppra, and lamotrigine
– Liver function monitoring and other labs
– Insurance/prior authorization, given my previous med trials

I know there are no guarantees, but even being seizure‑free long enough to meet the Ohio BMV requirements to reinstate my license would be life‑changing.

Thank you,
Emily Rose Carter
1437 Willow Bend Lane, Apt 3B
Columbus, OH 43202

metadata.subject: Goals for treatment & questions about Epidiolex

artifacts.content:
Riverside Family Dental Clinic
Employee Accommodation / Health Update Form (Internal)

Employee Name: Emily Rose Carter
Position: Part-time Office Assistant
Date: February 10, 2026

1. Brief health summary (employee-provided)
I have refractory focal epilepsy with occasional focal to bilateral tonic–clonic seizures. I’m followed by Dr. Arjun Mehta at OSU Wexner Medical Center and currently take levetiracetam (Keppra), clobazam (Onfi), and lamotrigine (Lamictal), with intranasal midazolam (Nayzilam) as rescue medication.

2. Impact on work
Most weeks I have 1–2 focal impaired-awareness seizures. After these, I’m usually confused and exhausted for 20–30 minutes and may need to step away from the front desk. About once or twice a month I have larger seizures that sometimes require EMS or an ER visit at OhioHealth Riverside Methodist Hospital. I do not drive and rely on COTA buses or family.

3. Current accommodations that help
- Shift schedule of 9:30 AM–3:00 PM on Mon/Wed/Fri
- Flexibility to sit down between phone bursts
- Coworkers aware of basic seizure first aid

4. Additional request
I’m working with my neurologist on possibly adding Epidiolex to reduce seizure frequency and eventually meet the seizure‑free interval required by the Ohio BMV so I can reapply for my driver’s license. My long‑term goal is to be able to drive myself to work instead of depending on my sister Hannah for rides.

5. Emergency plan at work
If I have a seizure:
- Keep me safe from injury
- Do NOT put anything in my mouth
- If convulsions last >5 minutes, administer Nayzilam (in my bag) and call 911.

Employee Signature: ____________________
Manager (Denise) Signature: _____________
